title: Lesson 229
transcript: Music playing Now we've been talking about how it's important to protect assets as they go through their entire life cycle from the time that we create or acquire assets to the time they are destroyed at some point in the future and obviously what's important is that we first of all know what assets we have but the second step is to identify ownership because the owners are always in the best position to understand the value of the asset that really starts the asset classification process itself. But this is where a lot of companies actually fail. and that is in determining who the actual owners are so if you were to ask for example who is the owner that sometimes is a very difficult question to answer but typically the owner is the person or the function or the system for example that creates the asset in the first place or acquires it or inherits it for example and because of that they're always going to be in the best position to understand the value that drives how we protect it and of course it's the owners that start this process of asset classification because that is going to be based on the value of the asset at all times. Now this is where companies fail as I said before you know establishing who the owner is and getting them to understand that entire process of first of all classifying properly and then ensuring that that asset goes through its life cycle and is protected as it goes through that life cycle is something that the owners are always accountable for. Now I know we've talked about the difference between the word accountability and responsibility and we've said many times that accountability is something that you cannot delegate. to anyone else you can delegate the responsibility but protecting assets requires the owner to always remain accountable for protecting those assets even though they are going to give access for example to custodians and other users, but they need to start the process itself. And so one of the things that we need to understand is that because we've been talking about asset protection, That means the owners of those assets need to start this process of classification so that we can protect the assets properly. But there are different types of assets that exist in the organization. And so this slide here is supposed to show you that it's not just data owners that we need, for example. we need to establish ownership for any of the assets that represent value to the organization. And so you've got process owners, you've got hardware owners, you've got function owners, you've got application owners, you've got system owners, and yes, we also have data owners. but the accountability that they all have is exactly the same. And that is to understand the value of those assets that represent value to the organization and therefore, classify those assets properly so that they can be protected as they go through obviously their life cycle and we protect as it goes through those life cycle. Now we've seen questions on the exam that reflects something that you see on your slide here and that is the different roles that may exist in an organization and whether they are accountable or responsible for protecting those assets and I think this is pretty intuitive but we're going to talk about it here very briefly so the owners and again it's not just data owners that we're talking about here in fact that should say asset owner but the reason it says data owner is because that's probably what you're going to see in some of the questions that you'll be subjected to. Owners are always accountable for protecting what they own and they start the process of asset classification and then obviously remain accountable if that asset is used by others within the organization. The processor, this could be your IT function, for example. And so the IT function would be responsible for ensuring that what they own, and that could be technology, is also protected to the level that we require. And they become responsible at that point for protecting the information that may be processed on their systems and their assets. The next one down is the custodian. And I think we've said earlier before that the custodian is responsible. Someone that has custody of an asset that doesn't really belong to them. They remain responsible. Another name for a custodian would be users because they are using those assets through their life cycle and therefore they become responsible for protecting those assets while they are in their position. The data steward might be, for example, the administrator of a system that is responsible for understanding the data formats that that data is represented in in that architecture. and so they are also responsible for protecting certain things related to the formats that the information may be in. And the data subject, that's the person that we collect information on, and we process that information on and so this is related to something that we talked about earlier and that is privacy when companies collect information that relates to their customers and their shareholders that information belongs to the data subject and because the company has collected that information The subject is now removed from having the opportunity to dictate, for example, what security needs to look like within the organization. But that's why we have privacy laws and legislation that are imposed on organizations that really ensure that even though the data subject is removed from being able to dictate how to protect their information, all of that is dictated through laws and regulations that we refer to as privacy legislation, part of compliance for most organizations.
